sentences.text: Well, you asked several times about life in the ghetto, either in Lwow or in Rawa-Ruska.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Probably to understand this, you have to accept the fact that there was no normalcy of any kind.
Nothing was predictable or stable.
Danger was looming at every moment, in every corner.
There was no protection of any law or any authority.
A Jew was free victim and prey to everybody.
So it was a life of a hunted animal, either hunted by the Germans, or by the Ukrainian police, or by local populace.
This is one aspect of this problem.
The second aspect of the problem was a terrible hunger, starvation, because food was not available.
Or it was available, and local population was selling food to Jews for gold, or precious things, anything, clothes, depriving these poor people of what was left to them.
Third problem, by moving from place to place, they left their possessions behind, because you couldn't carry it.
And the space was smaller and smaller.
So try to imagine here, people who were constantly squeezed and under pressure.
So your questions about celebrations, education, and so forth, don't apply.
Everybody--
But they did happen.
People did get married.
People did have children.
They got married because, you see, people in those days have a concept of correctness and dignity.
And people wouldn't live together unless it was done in a traditional way by having a ceremony.
End of story.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Women got pregnant and gave birth.
And people died.
People were sick.
The only circumstances-- the circumstances were extraordinary.
Punished by whom?
For what?
Not really.
There was some criminal police and Jewish criminal police who dealt with criminals, but only to a certain point.
I don't believe that there was anything of that sort.
The Jews were criminals too.
Well, thieves, pickpockets, murderers, anything you want.
Surely.
No.
It was practically impossible to escape.
Now, first of all, I've seen the horror of Brygidki, so I realized the full extent of the brutality, danger, and lawlessness.
Secondly, as a child, I was usually more independent and more resourceful than the usual Jewish child, which was fearful and brought up to keep away from Gentiles.
So this, you see, gave me a much greater initiative.
And thirdly, I was privileged because people in the ghetto said, you are very fortunate because you don't look Jewish.
You have an enormous asset.
[LAUGHS] And I spoke the language.
And that gave me maneuverability.
Having escaped to Rawa-Ruska, I could eat better because food was more accessible there.
From Lwow to Rawa-Ruska?
Very simply, I got myself as my wife's-- my mother's cousin-- this job in [NON-ENGLISH], and went there as a collector of rags.
End of story.
So you had a legal exit from-- Some-- well, surely.
And we arrived in Rawa-Ruska, and we lived there.
End of story.
And from the documents, you'll find that this was organized in a certain way.
And certain categories of people were left alone.
After that, the pressure started to build up.
And there were [? akcja, ?] what you call.
Akcja ?] or most essentially an organized collection of certain categories of people who were shipped to a death camp.
Oh, yes.
Actually, one--
I was in [? akcja, ?] two in Rawa-Ruska and two in Lwow.
Rawa-Ruska.
Yes and no.
It was not a defined ghetto.
No.
Rawa-Ruska is about-- not more than 18 kilometers from Belzitz.
And the camp-- the death camp in Bel-- it was not a concentration camp.
It was just simply, no more and no less, gas chambers and crematoria.
What people don't realize, that perhaps a million to a million and a half Jews were processed there.
That was not far from Rawa-Ruska, 18 kilometers.
The-- Belzitz death camp was erected, I think, in January, February 1942, shortly after Wannsee Conference.
And the first group of Jews who were caught and taken to be tested, as the construction was completed, were Jews from Rawa-Ruska, 200 of them.
Oh, yes.
We knew that they were killed there.
We didn't know the method because the Ukrainians were not allowed to go there either.
So they said that they killed Jews.
How?
There were all kinds of versions, by steam, by electricity, and so forth.
But they knew that they were killed there.
There was no [? selectia. ?]
They just simply came to town with trucks, with the [NON ENGLISH], which was the military police.
It's described in the book which was published here in the United States, Regiment 101 of the reserve police.
And they caught, at random, 200 people, put them on trucks, shipped them, and they disappeared.
End of story.
Yes.
[DRINKS]
Well, Germans were on the ground catching Jews with the help of the Ukrainian police and with the help of local population.
They were collected, usually, next to the railroad station and shipped wherever they were shipped.
Well, once-- in the second [? akcja ?] in Rawa-Ruska, we were hiding in the warehouse where the rags were kept inside-- in the rags.
And I went to visit somebody.
And a Ukrainian teenager grabbed me and started to drag me to the collection or to assembly area.
As I told you, that I was a scrappy youngster, so I beat him up and ran away.
In Lwow, the major [? akcja, ?] which probably was one of the largest, 60,000 people were collected.
We were hiding in the cellar in the building where my mother lived for a while after leaving her original home.
And this cellar, well, I ran out of there and went back to Rawa-Ruska because of arrangements, which I have described in my book.
My mother was sticking out of there because Polish women who were sitting there and selling vegetables pointed out to the Germans and to the Ukrainian police, the Jews are there.
They were all taken to assembly point.
My mother succeeded to pay off Jewish policemen with some jewelry she had.
And she and my brothers were returned to the ghetto.
Hundreds of them, but I think-- [LAUGHS]
The fear was very simply to be caught and be brought to a situation, which I found myself in Brygidki.
I kept my wits with-- and I was alert.
I watched around-- watched what is happening around me and lived like an animal, a hunted animal.
Period.
End.
There are not one.
There are very many.
One was to be shipped to be shot in Lwow, to Piaski, with other members of the Judenrat.
Two--
I told you.
I jumped out of a truck.
Two, I was coming back from a visit in Rawa-Ruska through side streets, and I run into two SS men who were passing-- these were Waffen-SS-- passing through Rawa-Ruska.
It was a railroad center.
And they accosted me and asked me what am I doing.
Well, I said, I am a member of the [NON-ENGLISH] polizei, which means Jewish police health department.
And I'm coming back from the hospital were I transported typhoid sick people.
When they heard typhoid, they ran away, but this was a pretty close.
I was lucky they didn't take a pistol-- one of them didn't shoot me on the spot.
It's very--
First of all, the estate of my grandfather is about, probably, 12 kilometers from Belzitz.
Belzitz had a sugar beet refinery.
And I used to go there with shipments of sugar beet, because it was interesting to observe it.
Therefore, I knew the topography of the area, and I knew that Belzitz is a dead end.
Every day, I could see 10 or 20 trains coming from the direction of Lwow, going to Belzitz, and coming back empty.
There was no space, you see, for these people to be collected there and shipped, what they called, resettlement.
So it was quite clear that something is happening to them.
That's what's triggered, in my mind the attempt to escape and looking for ways, which was very difficult.
Some people forged documents.
Some were caught and shot.

So it was a rather frightening experience to get out of the ghetto because this illusion that if you observe the law and the regulations, you might survive, this was something which was fed to these people all the time, by the Judenrats, by the [NON-ENGLISH], like [NON-ENGLISH], or Teicholz.
I don't remember.
Mhm.
He was here in New York.
I, I read an obituary of his death two or three years ago.
First of all, I went to Lwow to consult with my mother.
And then I told her that I would like to see the Metropolitan Sheptytsky, and at least safe guard the documents of my father, which she allowed me to do.
The archbishop.
Yes.
It was probably two miles, in the city though.
Oh, yes.
They received me very warmly.
And-- I described it in my book-- and asked me who I am.
And he was very moved when he learned that I am the son of my father, because he was aware that he was killed.
And he said, documents, documents.
But I think I would like to save you and others.
He was aware of what's going on.
And he asked me to come back after three or four days.
No.
When I came back after three days, he told me that he would like to take my, my brother, who was, at that time, probably eight years old or nine years old, and place him with monks.
And to discuss it with my mother and bring him to the palace.
Well, my mother was not overly enthusiastic.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But eventually, she realized, because this was after the major actions, that, sooner or later, it will end badly.
She was already on her way to Belzitz, so she agreed.
So did my brother.
So the next time, we took him there.
Myself, my mother, and my younger brother, who was nine years old.
And we were received in the office of the Metropolitan of the archbishop.
There was a priest waiting.
He promised my mother that he would take care of him, be his guardian.
And the priest took my brother, Father Kiprion.
And that's the end of it.
And I returned with my mother alone to the ghetto.
Oh, yes.
Later on.
Until I went to visit my brother in the monastery where he lived.
I went there.
It's a place called [NON-ENGLISH], which is about, again, 70 miles from Lwow.
Well, I came back.
My mother told me, look, this boy is nine years old, and he cannot manage by himself.
You have to go.
And so I agreed to it.
And I spoke to-- again, to the Metropolitan.
It was fine.
And I understood that if I get out, my mother will be eventually rescued.
But she refused.
And so I went away.
That was sometime October 1942, about maybe a month and and a half before the ghetto was closed.
Very simple.
[LAUGHS] I just walked to the palace with a little valise, a few of my belongings.
And I came out of there without the Jewish arm band.
And I was taken to an orphanage, where I was placed for a few days, and then taken to another monastery.
And that's it.
All the monks knew that I'm Jewish.
Probably.
Later on, I found out, yes, quite a number.
No.
They were teenagers, 12 to 16.
Nothing.
But this was a temporary place until-- you see, to place a Jew was not so easy-- [LAUGHS] --In those days.
A reason being-- sorry to be vulgar-- but in Europe, Jews, only Jews are circumcised.
Gentiles are not.
And therefore, they didn't bother with any documents.
Consequently, to place a Jew was not an easy business.
Yes.
Nuns took girls.
I found girls eventually too.
But that's much later.
People didn't advertise it because for-- the penalty for saving a Jew or keeping a Jew was death.
Therefore, the whole thing was done very discreetly.
Now, who were your contacts in this orphanage?
How long were you in the orphanage?
About two or three days.
They sent me to next door.
About maybe a kilometer from there was a man's monastery there, John the Baptist Monastery.
There, I was kept for a month.
No.
I was already given this false name, [NON-ENGLISH].
And I was kept locked up in this cell.
My only contact was, was a priest, monk priest, who was teaching me language.
I didn't speak Ukrainian, custom, culture, how to pray, and things of that sort.
So I was, in a way, quickly trained to be adapted to my surrounding.
Well, I used to go to the refectory to have meals with the monks.
I could wander around the monastery.
In the beginning, maybe not, because they thought that maybe I'm a novice.
Then, slowly and gradually, it became clear to them.
At that time I had my own clothing.
No, the food-- I ate the food of the monastery.
Of what?
Well, no.
This, you see--
No.
[LAUGHS]
[LAUGHS] --The question was not, you see, to have kosher food, but to have food to survive the day, first of all.
And secondly, the monks did not worry about providing me with kosher food.
No.
You couldn't, because, you see, I was observed all the time.
The minute I would start-- [LAUGHS] --You see, any religious practices, I would endanger the whole community and myself.
Myself.
My false name was [NON-ENGLISH].
Not really.
I knew that this is my name.
And the same way, my brother knew that he had his name.
It was different from mine in order not to make any connection in case either of us is caught.
Were you in the same monastery?
I was in constant fear, constant fear.
So were they.
[LAUGHS] They were afraid that if I am caught, they will be shot.
[LAUGHS] How does one learn to be a Ukrainian?
Very simple.
You see, as long as you realize that your life depends on it, you learn very quickly.
Well, that I was taught, and I'm a very quick learner.
So I had to learn, first of all, mannerisms.
I have to-- I had to learn, very quickly, the language, and the ways of Ukrainian prayers, and behave in the church.
I never went to the sacraments, but I had to make the right moves and the rhythm of what was going on.
I had-- I am not a Catholic.
And I didn't want to-- respect-- they told me not to go to sacraments because of blasphemy, because they believe that the host is the body and-- of Christ.
Not really.
It was not in my mind at that time because the question was the survival from day to day, from hour to hour.
I was in a variety of monasteries.
No, but the first one.
The first one, I would say probably close to 30.
Probably two months or three months.

After three months, I was sufficiently trained that I could sing in the choir with them, learned old Slavonic, because they play in old Slavonic, and participate in all of the ceremonies without any problem.
They treat me-- they treated me the same way as they treat me today.
In their minds, I am one of them.
And they treat me-- treated me as a friend, as part of the community.
No, I was treated the same way as I-- as everybody else.
I worked with them, as everybody else, which was not easy.
It was a very hard life, very difficult.
We faced the danger the same way.
And all I can say, curiously enough, I said in-- old conscience today, from perspective of 71 years of life and other experience, these were the happiest days of my life.
Why?

I lived with people who were decent, who tried to live by the gospels-- I didn't say that they were perfect-- who provided me with shelter and never made me feel that I'm an outsider, who accepted the same risk of being caught and shot, which faced me by the simple fact that I'm there.
And while there was a storm all around me, I was, in a way, like in a Noah's arc.
Were you aware that there were other Jews there?
In the beginning, no.
Slowly and gradually, because this was a silent order.
And we communicated only half an hour a day in the circle of recreation.
And then gradually, they let me know indirectly.
Well--
No.
Monks.
Yes.
Oh, just hinted.
One saw my brother somewhere, and he is well.
Another mentioned that the Jew is sitting in the cellar.
A third one told me that this German priest, Father Peters, keeps four Jewish families-- [LAUGHS] --In the cellar of the factory which he run, and so forth.
And all of this was-- and one day I met a monk.
He was Brother Daniel with a beard.
And I asked him-- he didn't know that I'm Jewish because I already looked-- he was not in the same monastery as I was.
So I asked him, Father Daniel, what are you doing?
Well, he says, I'm, I'm running a [NON-ENGLISH].
He had-- he ran an orphanage.
And in this orphanage, there were three Ukrainian children and 17 Jewish children, boys.
Absolutely.
Well, every Ukrainian knows what the [NON-ENGLISH] is.
They lived together, you see, for centuries.
No, I've never encountered any homosexuality.
None.
It's only a figment of the imagination of the sick, Western mind, especially American mind.
I lived in a monastery for two years in very close quarters.
I'm sorry to disappoint you.
I wasn't molested, and I haven't see anything, which I'm a pretty keen observer, as you can gather.
Oh, yes.
[LAUGHS]
What?
For the next tape?
Surely.
